summary: This source is an abstract for a paper co-authored by Judea Pearl. It introduces machine learning models (Exact-MLP and Mask-MLP) designed to estimate bounds for probabilities of causation, such as the probability of necessity and sufficiency (PNS), overcoming the unreliability of obtaining subpopulation-specific experimental and observational data.

principles.statement: Probabilities of causation are essential for modern decision-making.
principles.rationale: They provide formal definitions and bounds (such as the probability of necessity and sufficiency) that are central to evaluating decisions and outcomes.
principles.source_quote: Probabilities of causation play a central role in modern decision making.

principles.statement: Machine learning can overcome the data limitations inherent in estimating probabilities of causation.
principles.rationale: By training models on a small set of reliable subpopulations, it is possible to accurately predict causal probability bounds for other subpopulations where data is limited or unreliable.
principles.source_quote: These results demonstrate both the feasibility of machine learning models for learning probabilities of causation and the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

frameworks.name: Mask-Augmented ML for PNS Bounds
frameworks.when_to_apply: When estimating probabilities of causation (like PNS) but experimental and observational distributions for specific subpopulations are unreliable or impractical to obtain from limited population-level data.
steps: Identify a small set of reliable subpopulations with sufficient experimental and observational data.
Train machine learning models (Exact-MLP and Mask-MLP) on this reliable subset.
Use the trained models to predict PNS bounds for all other subpopulations.
frameworks.source_quote: we propose two machine learning models: Exact-MLP and Mask-MLP, which are trained on a small set of reliable subpopulations and are able to predict PNS bounds for all other subpopulations.

mental_models.name: Structural Causal Models (SCMs)
mental_models.description: A mathematical framework used to represent causal relationships and generate population-level data, serving as a ground-truth environment to validate causal inference algorithms and machine learning models.
mental_models.example: Validating the Exact-MLP and Mask-MLP models across four different SCMs using population-level data with sample sizes between 100k and 200k.

signature_quotes.text: Probabilities of causation play a central role in modern decision making.
signature_quotes.context: Opening statement of the abstract, establishing the importance of the paper's focus.

anti_patterns.description: Relying strictly on subpopulation-specific experimental and observational distributions to estimate probabilities of causation when population-level data is limited.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It is often unreliable or impractical to obtain this specific data for every subpopulation, making direct estimation impossible without predictive modeling.
